To solve this problem without becoming a custodian, he realized he had to stop viewing Hyperliquid as an exchange built on a blockchain and start viewing it as a blockchain with built-in exchange functionality. The blockchain the team built to run the exchange, already capable of processing hundreds of thousands of orders per second, could now be made programmable. This would be an open system where anyone could write code and build financial applications, just like on Ethereum. The difference is that Ethereum is too slow to run a real exchange, which is why Yan built his own chain in the first place. If he opens this chain, assets can enter Hyperliquid through decentralized bridges protected by the protocol itself, without any single party acting as custodian. Anyone building on the programmable layer can directly access the exchange's order book and all its liquidity. Developers can build lending platforms, stablecoins, or mobile trading apps and directly access a market where professional companies quote billions of dollars daily. Yan doesn't like analogies. He'd tell you that Hyperliquid has no equivalent in traditional finance, and that people are used to cramming new things into old categories instead of understanding them logically, which he sees as a mistake. But for those of us who aren't "Yan," it's like Amazon building its cloud service to support its e-commerce business, only to realize that the cloud service is much larger than e-commerce. The phrase Yan first used in that tweet was: Hyperliquid will "accommodate all financial transactions." He had previously been reluctant to make this change. He told me that subconsciously he didn't want to take on the task. Building a virtual machine in Hyperliquid is a massive undertaking; the team didn't know if it was feasible or how much work would need to be done from scratch. But at some point, he said it was obvious. If they didn't, they would spend years piecing together something that resembled both Binance and Ethereum, but neither, and they would regret it later. The community was furious. They expected a token airdrop, but instead saw a tweet about infrastructure. A comment with thousands of likes quoted a line from Breaking Bad: “We were doing just fine.” “I hate this, you betrayed us.” Users didn’t want blockchain; they wanted money. Xulian, who joined the team after a user interview that was supposed to be 15 minutes but ended up lasting an hour and a half and never really finished, absorbed this anger. “Yan is thinking about the best long-term interests,” he told me. “We really don’t care if something looks good right now.” As “iliensinc” described, the loudest voices eventually get tired. The team spent the next six months refining the spot functionality, building the programmable layer, testing on a standalone network, and preparing for staking. Then, on November 29th, a Friday, **HYPE** arrived. Hyperliquid airdropped 31% of its total token supply to approximately 94,000 early adopters. There were no strings attached, and no vesting period. If you had used the platform and earned credits, you woke up that morning with a large amount of tokens in your wallet, much richer than you were when you went to sleep. Based on the opening price, this airdrop was worth over $1 billion; based on its all-time high, it would reach $16 billion. This is the largest wealth transfer in cryptocurrency history, with every dollar flowing to users. The team's own allocation was 23.8%, smaller than the community's share, and it would be unlocked gradually over several years. On the day of the token issuance, they received nothing. VCs didn't receive a penny either. If they wanted tokens, they had to buy them on the open market at the same price as everyone else on Hyperliquid, because they weren't listed anywhere else. That was the price you had to pay. That morning, Yan didn't need to explain anything on Twitter. "Woke up to a six-figure airdrop," one user wrote. Another replied, “HYPE changed my life today. This money is enough for me to live comfortably for years, help my family, and invest heavily in this bull market.” Someone else said, “A seven-figure airdrop, God bless Jeff.” “I feel great,” Yan told me. “There are very few cases where early participants can share in the profits and gain substantial ownership of the network.” I asked him how he felt now that everything they built now had such a public price. “Terrible,” he said. It was a Wednesday evening in late March 2025 when “iliensinc”’s computer started alarming. She was on a call and then hung up. On the screen, the balance of the Hyperliquid Community Vault (HLP) was falling. A trader had been probing Hyperliquid’s defenses with small, coordinated positions for the past few days. Now, the probing was over. They opened three positions on Jelly Jelly, a niche token with a market capitalization of approximately $15 million and a daily trading volume of only $72,000. One was a large short position, and two were long positions. The short position was destined to fail; the trader was shorting a token they were about to pump, and when the short position was liquidated, someone had to take over. It's like pulling the pin on a grenade and handing it to someone else. The person who took over was HLP. On Hyperliquid, when the order book can't handle a trader's closing orders, the community treasury takes over the position and gradually liquidates it over time. Under normal circumstances, this is standard practice. But Jelly Jelly has almost no depth, and while HLP was trapped and unable to exit, the trader was frantically buying on the open market. In less than an hour, the price surged by over 500%. As the price fluctuated, the treasury's losses continued to grow. "iliensinc" stared at the screen. Losses exceeded $5 million, then $8 million, then $12 million. There was no mechanism in the system to stop it. No one had anticipated that someone would use a $15 million token as a weapon. Validators across Asia and Europe went live. Hyperliquid's blockchain was secured by about twenty independent operators. They validated every transaction and gained voting power by staking large amounts of HYPE as collateral. Many of them had been using Hyperliquid before the token existed. They could see everything happening on the public ledger and didn't consider it a legitimate transaction. Within minutes, everyone voted to remove Jelly Jelly and settle at the price before the manipulation began. Every user with a legitimate position received a full refund; the only one who lost money was the attacker. He realized he had to stop viewing Hyperliquid as an exchange built on a blockchain and start viewing it as a blockchain with built-in exchange functionality. This incident raises a question that Hyperliquid critics have long wanted to ask: if a dozen or so validators can cover market prices and set their own settlement prices, how decentralized is the system? Yan didn't shy away from the question. The small validator set is intentionally designed. A system that upgrades every few weeks can't coordinate thousands of participants with each upgrade. The set will expand over time, but not at the expense of the "speed" that made Hyperliquid successful. "The fix took a month. It feels bad to learn this by being attacked instead of being reminded," Yan said. Hyperliquid never pays market makers or takes a cut from the team, but it will pay up to $1 million for a bug report. "But these people obviously weren't trying to tell us about the problem; they wanted to exploit it." At the time of the attack, the world's two largest centralized exchanges, Binance and OKX, had also launched Jelly Jelly perpetual contracts on their respective platforms. On Twitter, a user tagged Binance co-CEO He Yi, urging her to list the token. “If you list Jelly Jelly,” they wrote, “Hyperliquid is probably doomed.” He Yi replied in Chinese: “Okay, got it.” This is the reward for ambition. You left a Puerto Rican beach where nobody knew you. You built from scratch with your own TV and savings. You turned down $100 million. You gave billions to strangers. And what did you get? War. In 2023 and 2024, Hyperliquid was small and nobody paid attention. The airdrop changed everything. A market cap of $4.2 billion, $9 billion, or even higher meant that every major company in the crypto space could see this future: Hyperliquid had taken their jobs. Binance announced its own decentralized exchange. Coinbase and Robinhood began offering futures products. New protocols launched targeting Hyperliquid. Then, someone followed Yan into his apartment elevator. This may have been a false alarm, but in 2025, violent attacks against cryptocurrency holders nearly doubled. In France, the co-founder of a hardware wallet company had a finger sawed off, and his photo was sent to his partners as ransom. In Canada, a family was waterboarded. Cryptocurrency transfers are instantaneous, irreversible, and require no bank approval. A person with a wrench and a wallet address can drain a fortune. Yan moved to a safer place, hired bodyguards, and was somewhat confined to one of the safest island cities on Earth. He was accompanied by two private bodyguards when he traveled. “iliensinc” began testing team members on how to answer if a stranger asked where they worked. This is why almost everyone appearing in this profile uses a pseudonym. When I asked Yan what the toughest moment of 2025 was, he didn't mention Jelly Jelly, his competitors, or his bodyguards. He told me it was the API server. Throughout the summer, as Bitcoin broke $100,000, Hyperliquid was processing over $400 billion in transactions monthly, and the servers connecting market makers to the blockchain began to strain. Too many companies were joining, each sending massive amounts of orders, cancellations, and updates, and the infrastructure forwarding all of this to the chain couldn't keep up. An order that should have been cleared instantly took three seconds. The chain itself didn't crash, and user funds were never at risk. But in a market where profits and losses are measured in milliseconds, three seconds is a warning sign. "If we don't experience congestion during extremely volatile moments," said "iliensinc," "then when those extreme moments actually happen, it's unacceptable." Yan suffered from insomnia for weeks. He would go to bed at 1:30 AM and be woken up at 3:00 AM by messages saying "things are broken again." The team completely rewrote the servers from the ground up. On October 11th, that moment happened. President Trump's threat to impose 100% tariffs on Chinese imports triggered the liquidation of over $19 billion in leveraged cryptocurrency positions within 24 hours, marking the industry's largest crash in history. Over 1.6 million traders were caught in a self-reinforcing chain reaction: forced sell-offs drove down prices, triggering more liquidations, which in turn drove prices down further. Hyperliquid didn't crash, nor did it halt withdrawals. The rewritten servers held up, as did Jelly Jelly's recovery mechanism. HLP, acting as a backer, liquidated billions of positions and earned $40 million from it. But because every transaction on the Hyperliquid blockchain is public, anyone can count its liquidation volume. Other exchanges report liquidation volumes far less precisely; Binance releases only one per second. The data aggregators relied upon by major media outlets use off-the-shelf data, which is misleading. Media reports claimed Hyperliquid handled more liquidations than any other exchange. It appears to be the most dangerous trading venue for a simple reason: it's the most transparent. Three days later, while others in the crypto space were still cleaning up the mess, Yan's team released an upgrade that defined Hyperliquid's future: **HIP-3**. HIP-3 allowed anyone who staked 500,000 HYPE tokens to deploy new perpetual contract markets on the platform, setting their own parameters, choosing their own price sources, and retaining half of the transaction fees. By the end of the year, its second full year of operation, Hyperliquid had earned approximately $900 million in profit. The team took nothing. 99% of the profits were automatically converted into HYPE and burned, permanently withdrawing from circulation, returning almost all of the platform's revenue to every token holder. When I asked "iliensinc" how she would look back on 2025, she said, "It feels like we've grown up." On my last afternoon in the office, Yan and I sat at the black dining table next to the kitchen, within earshot of those untouched whiskeys—the place where the team ate lunch together every day. I asked some questions that had been left unanswered until the very end. Hyperliquid has been relinquishing some of its power over the past year. The "Builder Code," launched before HIP-3, allowed independent developers to build trading apps on the platform's order book and share every transaction fee generated by users. Matt Huang, co-founder of Paradigm (one of the largest investment firms in the crypto space), called it "a brilliant way to franchise the user experience." Since October 2024, these teams have earned over $70 million. HIP-3 went even further. In the six months since its launch, seven independent teams have deployed hundreds of markets, most of which are unrelated to cryptocurrencies: crude oil, gold, stock indices, and forex. The largest deployer, Trade[XYZ], has grown 38% weekly since October 2025. It has handled over $130 billion in volume across 192,000 traders. Markets created by independent deployers now account for half of Hyperliquid's total trading volume. In February 2026, HIP-4 was announced. When it launches, anyone will be able to deploy options or prediction markets on the platform. HIP-3 opened Hyperliquid to any asset with a price, while HIP-4 will open it to any event with a result. The most important products on Hyperliquid are currently being developed by people who don't work for Yan and never will. I asked him how he viewed this: what should his team build, and what should they leave for others? "It's a dynamic question, and I don't think there's a standard answer," he said. "The most important angle is philosophical. Are you building a financial super app like Robinhood, or are you building a financial system?" He admitted he didn't know which would win. “But I think an accessible financial system is a better outcome for the world. A system that operates on a public track, not belonging to any single company. To build it, we often thought: what do we need to do to enable others to succeed on Hyperliquid and own their own businesses? When people start competing and owning their own things, a more resilient and scalable system emerges.” He said the easiest path would be to build everything ourselves and keep it within one company. But they chose the opposite path. “It’s the hardest path to do things, but we care a lot about the path to our goal because how we get there determines what we ultimately build.” The founder of Trade[XYZ] told me he feels that perhaps one day, people won’t even know they’re using Hyperliquid. “Perhaps in its final form, it will simply be the infrastructure and liquidity layer of the financial industry,” he said, “while companies like Interactive Brokers, Phantom Wallet, and anyone else will ultimately interact directly with users. That’s actually quite nice.” Huang’s company, Paradigm, invested heavily in the public market shortly after the HYPE token launched. “It was incredible,” he told me, “because all of this was built by a team of only 11 people.” 11 people, and almost no AI. In the office, there were dedicated AI laptops running the latest models, but they were only used for exploring ideas. “We carefully monitor the AI’s capabilities,” Yan said, “It’s not good enough to write important code yet.” At the opening price, that airdrop was worth over $1 billion; at its all-time high, it reached $16 billion. This was the largest wealth transfer in cryptocurrency history, with every dollar flowing to users. I asked Yan about the biggest cloud hanging over all of this. Since 2023, Hyperliquid's cumulative trading volume has exceeded $4 trillion. It holds a 37% share of the decentralized perpetual contract market. And all of this has been accomplished without the reach of the world's largest capital market users. Americans are excluded. The obstacle lies in the Dodd-Frank Act, a U.S. law passed after the 2008 financial crisis that requires every derivatives transaction to go through a regulated intermediary. Ironically, Hyperliquid's public ledger has already provided regulators with what the Dodd-Frank Act was intended to produce: real-time visibility into all leverage in the system. But until the Commodity Futures Trading Commission (CFTC) establishes new rules, Americans have no legal path to trade derivatives through decentralized protocols. True to his philosophy, Yan did not personally build a policy team. A month after my visit, the Hyperliquid Policy Center was established as an independent nonprofit organization, led by Jake Chervinsky, a renowned lawyer with a decade of experience in the crypto space. The Hyper Foundation, an independent body supporting the growth of the Hyperliquid ecosystem, donated 1 million HYPE tokens (worth $28 million) as its seed funding. Yan acknowledged that Hyperliquid had reached a certain scale, meaning that a "wait and see" approach was no longer a viable strategy. “Some people are lobbying in the opposite direction,” he told me. “I can’t be too confident about the final outcome. But regulation ultimately reflects the will of the people, and I’m optimistic about its direction.” I had one last question I’d been holding onto all week: You don’t really think Hyperliquid will accommodate all financial business, do you? He laughed, and for a man who cuts his own hair, he laughed more than you’d expect. “What I mean is, I think the word ‘all’ has a bit of a superlative connotation,” he said. “That’s our vision. But it’s really hard to achieve, and setting goals that span decades is very arrogant.” “That’s the difference between Go and chess,” he continued. “In chess, the higher your skill level, the more moves you can see. But in Go, there are too many possibilities. The focus is on developing an intuition for the next move, not trying to see through the entire decision tree.” I probably looked like I needed more explanation, so he rephrased it. He always tried to follow this principle: believe you’re moving in the right direction and execute each move well, without having to know exactly where you’re going. The next evening, a Friday, the team went to a Chinese restaurant in a city hotel. The engineer who “colonized the office with plush toys” couldn’t come, but everyone else was there, including me. We were led through a quiet lobby, down a corridor, and into a private room with dark wood paneling and carved lattice screens, furnished with a round table. Behind a screen at the far end of the private room were several armchairs surrounding a coffee table, where we sat down to drink tea. The room was cold; the air conditioning was set lower than it should be for the night. Someone handed the youngest engineer a blanket, which he draped over his shoulders, discovering it was a Christian Dior. This sparked a discussion between him and Yan about luxury brands, neither of them clearly having any background in the field. One of them pronounced LVMH as "LHVM," and neither corrected the other. "iliensinc," wearing a Ralph Lauren hat, sighed. As we sat down to eat, the lazy Susan began to spin, almost without stopping. Dishes were placed one after another along the edge of the lazy Susan until a large blue-and-white porcelain bowl was brought out, and the table fell silent. The bowl contained shallow water covered with pebbles and fine leaves—a miniature koi pond. A bowl of pristine white noodles sat in the center, surrounded by three small, orange-red fish swimming around it, circling in the "moat" between the two bowls. The waiter explained the dish to us. He said these fish had rested for 30 days, only to "work" for these 5 minutes. We watched them swim in circles, then they were removed, beginning another month-long vacation. We left around 9:15 p.m., walking into the drizzle. I said goodbye and got into a taxi to the airport. A few minutes after leaving the hotel, the taxi began climbing a long left-turn ramp onto the highway. Turning the corner, the financial district came into view: HSBC, JPMorgan Chase, Standard Chartered, Deutsche Bank, Citibank—their logos gleaming in the dark night sky. Then the road straightened eastward, the buildings receding one after another behind me until only the wet pavement remained in the rearview mirror. Yan headed in the opposite direction, back to his post, back to where he had left his bodyguard.

data.title: US cryptocurrency legislation passes another hurdle.
data.description:
Author: Guo Liqin, Researcher at Caijing

After more than half a year of undercurrents, US cryptocurrency legislation is once again facing a critical moment.

On April 13, 2026 local time, the US Senate will end its Easter recess. The week prior, US Treasury Secretary and other politicians, the cryptocurrency industry, and active members of Congress began increasing pressure on Congress to advance the "Clarity Act of 2025" (hereinafter referred to as the "Clarity Act").

In July 2025, US President Trump officially signed the National Innovation Act for Stablecoins (GENIUS Act) at the White House. This act sets rules at the federal level for the issuance and operation of dollar-backed payment stablecoins. The act became law on the same day it was signed. On the same day, the Clarity Act and the Anti-CBDC Surveillance State Act were also passed by the House of Representatives and transferred to the Senate for the next stage of the legislative process. Compared to the already enacted GENIUS Act and the concise Anti-CBDC Surveillance State Act, the cryptocurrency industry particularly desired the Clarity Act. The Clarity Act not only covers all digital assets associated with blockchain, but also transfers the main regulatory power over cryptocurrencies other than stablecoins to a smaller regulatory agency with fewer enforcement resources—the Commodity Futures Trading Commission (CFTC). This means that, from a legal perspective, it will completely reverse the high-pressure regulation of cryptocurrencies during the Biden administration. However, time is running out for Congress. Generally speaking, legislation is more difficult to advance in the U.S. Senate than in the House of Representatives. The Memorial Day recess in Congress begins on May 21st. If the Clarity Act fails to make significant progress in the Senate before then, the legislative process may fall into the political game of the midterm elections, facing even greater uncertainty. It is widely believed that the next few weeks are a crucial window of opportunity for the Clarity Act to move forward. Sun Yuanzhao, a scholar in the U.S., told Caixin that the pace and timing of pushing forward the Clarity Act are similar to Trump's push for the GENIUS Act, but the situation is more urgent. If the Clarity Act cannot pass before Congress's summer recess (before August), it is very likely to be stillborn. "Next, lawmakers will be busy with the midterm elections and won't have time to deal with this." Bitcoin's price movement can serve as a side reference for the cryptocurrency legislative process. Since Trump won the US election in November 2024, the price of Bitcoin has surged from approximately $70,000 to an all-time high of $126,000 in October 2025. However, since February 2026, due to factors such as the obstruction of the Clarity Act, the price of Bitcoin has successively fallen below the $80,000 and $70,000 marks, even briefly dipping to $60,000, a new low in nearly 16 months, leading to over $2 billion in liquidations across the cryptocurrency market within 24 hours. As of press time, the price of Bitcoin is above $70,000, but the prediction platform Polymarket shows that there is an 82% probability that Bitcoin will fall below $65,000 in 2026, and the probability of falling below $55,000 has also risen to about 60%. Bitcoin's price movement over the past year. Source: Internet. Should stablecoin holders be paid yields? A review of the upcoming voting schedule in the US Senate shows that there is currently no agenda item related to the Clarity Act. Sun Yuanzhao explained that the bill would not be included in the public agenda until the party caucuses had coordinated their efforts. "However, it's possible that the Senate, under pressure, might add a vote on the bill next week," he said. Cryptocurrencies generally fall into five categories: stablecoins, central bank digital currencies (CBDCs), financial products and real-world asset tokenization (RWA), tokens without off-chain asset backing (Bitcoin, Ethereum), and memes (Trumpcoin, Dogecoin). Over the past few months, the Clarity Act has faced multiple obstacles in the Senate. A heated debate has raged between banking groups and cryptocurrency industry groups, centered on whether to pay yields to stablecoin holders. Despite the GENIUS Act's explicit prohibition against regulated stablecoin issuers paying direct interest or returns, defining them as "payment stablecoins" similar to traditional currencies, many cryptocurrency platforms have found ways to circumvent regulations—offering rewards associated with stablecoin payment activities. The US banking industry maintains that these rewards are practically equivalent to traditional interest payments. Traditional financial institutions face stringent deposit insurance regulations and demanding capital requirements. Banks worry that unrestricted stablecoin rewards could lead to a significant outflow of funds from traditional savings accounts. Banking lobbying groups have been pushing for the Clarity Act to close the "third-party model loophole," preventing cryptocurrency exchanges, brokers, and affiliates from offering rewards or returns economically equivalent to interest on stablecoin balances. However, cryptocurrency practitioners argue that such a ban stifles innovation and reinforces the monopoly of traditional banks. The dispute between the two sides forced the legislative process of the Clarity Act to a halt this year. A balanced solution has emerged: allowing rewards for peer-to-peer payment activities while limiting passive income from stablecoin balances, aiming to both protect traditional bank deposits and promote innovation. However, on April 8th local time, the US government suddenly intervened, shifting its stance entirely to the cryptocurrency industry, adding uncertainty to the future of the bill. On April 8th local time, US Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent published an op-ed urging Congress to pass the Clarity Act. He believes the Clarity Act is a necessary supplement to the GENIUS Act, clarifying the regulatory approach for cryptocurrencies (or "digital assets") at the federal level. On the same day, the White House Council of Economic Advisers released a report stating that banning stablecoins from paying interest (including revenues offered through platforms and affiliates) would provide virtually no substantial boost to bank lending and would instead harm consumers' actual interests. This report was undoubtedly a powerful rebuttal to lobbying efforts by the banking industry. The US banking industry argued that pushing for stricter restrictions would better protect banks' deposit and loan businesses, especially smaller financial institutions, but the White House report refuted this argument with data. According to the White House's baseline model, extending the ban on stablecoin issuers under the GENIUS Act through the Clarity Act to exchanges and third parties would only increase total bank loans by $2.1 billion. This represents only 0.02% of total outstanding loans. Of this meager growth, approximately 76% will flow to the largest banks. Community banks with assets below $10 billion will see their lending capacity increase by only about $500 million, a mere 0.026% increase. The report argues that even considering all extreme scenarios—such as an explosive growth in stablecoins, all reserves locked in cash instead of Treasury bonds, and a major shift in Federal Reserve policy—the maximum increase in loans across the system would only be $531 billion, approximately 4.4% of total U.S. loans in the fourth quarter of 2025. For community banks, even in these unlikely scenarios, the best-case growth would be only 6.7%. Another potential obstacle to legislation is the Trump administration's desire for a swift passage of the Clarity Act, while refusing to accept any "ethical clauses" that could restrict Trump himself and his family's cryptocurrency business. The "ethics clause" refers to provisions pushed by some Democratic lawmakers to prohibit or restrict high-ranking U.S. officials from trading cryptocurrencies. The opportunity to successfully advance this legislation is slipping away, causing tension among those supporting it. In an article, U.S. Treasury Secretary Bessenter stated that, apart from stablecoins, the regulatory framework for the digital asset market remains unclear. The requirements previously issued by the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) and the Commodity Futures Trading Commission (CFTC) have overlapped and sometimes even contradicted each other, resulting in a lack of clear guidance for cryptocurrency developers, exchanges, and investors. The two regulatory agencies have taken different enforcement actions, and their regulatory objectives have shifted with the political climate. Bessant believes this uncertainty has foreseeable consequences. An increasing number of cryptocurrency development projects are moving to regions with clearer regulations, such as Singapore and Abu Dhabi, the capital of the UAE. In these regions, operators know when and how to register, what standards need to be met, and how to operate. "The benefits of registering in the US rarely outweigh the risks," Bessant said. Bessant also stated that by clarifying the scope of regulatory jurisdiction, the Clarity Act establishes a practical registration pathway for trading platforms and intermediaries, defining when digital assets are securities and when they are not. The Act also protects investors through disclosure and custody rules, introduces new measures to combat illicit finance, and grants new powers to address abuse. Wyoming Republican Senator Cynthia Lummis, a co-sponsor and advocate of the Senate's GENUIS Act and chair of the Senate Digital Assets Subcommittee, is known as the "Queen of Cryptocurrency." Since the Clarity Act was introduced, Lummis has been a strong supporter, repeatedly emphasizing its crucial role in preventing cryptocurrency companies from relocating outside the United States due to regulatory uncertainty. From April 10th to 11th, Lummis posted five consecutive messages on her social media accounts, urging Congress to pass the Clarity Act. "This is our last chance to pass the Clarity Act before 2030. We cannot gamble with America's financial future," Lummis warned in her posts. Given that Loomis's term as a congresswoman ends in January 2027, her warning carries even greater weight. This is because after the 2026 US midterm elections, the Democrats could potentially win back the House or Senate, thus preventing Trump's support for the cryptocurrency industry over the next two years. However, Trump himself has yet to exert pressure. In contrast, starting in March 2025, Trump has been publicly urging lawmakers to pass the GENUIS Act before the August recess that year. Another significant change is that on March 25, 2026, Trump's approval rating dropped from 40% the previous week to 36%, his lowest level since returning to the White House. Polls indicate that rising oil prices and widespread discontent over the US-Iran war are the main reasons for the decline in his approval rating. According to an analysis by the U.S. law firm Morgan Lewis, the Clarity Act is modeled in many ways on the 21st Century Financial Innovation and Technology Act (FIT21), which passed the House of Representatives in 2024 but failed to advance in the Senate. The Clarity Act grants the CFTC most jurisdiction over digital assets but retains some key responsibilities of the SEC (Securities and Exchange Commission), including jurisdiction over investment contracts involving digital commodities. The cryptocurrency industry believes that most cryptocurrencies should be classified as commodities rather than securities, thus avoiding compliance with a range of securities laws. During the previous Biden administration, the SEC argued that almost all cryptocurrencies were securities and should be subject to strict regulation. In 2023, the SEC sued the three largest cryptocurrency exchanges—Coinbase, Binance, and Kraken—launching a legal offensive that threatened the industry's survival. However, since Trump took office, the SEC has successively dropped or suspended lawsuits and investigations against more than ten crypto companies or individuals. In the United States, a bill must be passed by both the House of Representatives and the Senate in identical text form before it can become law after being signed by the President. According to the US Congressional website, the Senate received the Clarity Act on September 18, 2025. After two reviews, it was submitted to the Committee on Banking, Housing, and Urban Affairs. After several rounds of procedures, on January 12, 2026, the Senate Committee on Banking, Housing, and Urban Affairs released a 278-page draft bill for internal review. The draft bill prohibits digital asset service providers from offering users interest or returns solely for holding stablecoin balances, but allows for stablecoin rewards or activity-related incentives. This is the most controversial part of the draft bill. It is noteworthy that on January 21, 2026, the Senate Agriculture Committee released a draft of the Digital Goods Intermediaries Act, related to the Clarity Act. This draft was approved by the committee on January 29, 2026. According to the US legislative process, after the draft from the Banking, Housing, and Urban Affairs Committee passes its internal review, the two drafts must be reconciled and merged before being submitted to the full Senate for a vote. Any bill approved by the Senate must then be reconciled with (or replace) the version of the Clarity Act previously passed by the House of Representatives before it can be voted on in the House.

data.title: Hong Kong's stablecoins have taken their first step, but the real competition has only just begun.
data.description:
Author: Charlie Liu, Partner, Generative Ventures

Over the past year, although I've been based in the US, I've been deeply involved in the Hong Kong stablecoin process: I served as an advisor to an applicant institution, and was interviewed by Nikkei as a result.

Over the past few months, I've read many comments and analyses, talked to many people, and written several of my own judgments.

Last week, the first batch of licenses was finally issued, and the dust settled. Of course, I have mixed feelings.

Regardless of the outcome, this first step has finally been taken.

I.With the first batch of licenses issued, Hong Kong's first priority is to address "credible issuance"

For Hong Kong, a place that is accustomed to first considering the risks and then allowing the market to grow slowly, the fact that the first step can be implemented is more important than many beautiful narratives.

However, the first step is never the complete answer. On April 10th, the Hong Kong Monetary Authority (HKMA) officially issued the first batch of stablecoin issuer licenses to Anchorpoint Financial, led by Standard Chartered, and HSBC. A total of 36 companies applied in the first batch, but only 2 were approved. The HKMA's standards were very clear: risk control and compliance capabilities, a feasible business plan, a clear use case, and the principle of "similar activities, similar risks, similar regulations." This outcome may not be the most exciting for the market, but it almost perfectly reflects Hong Kong's institutional style. Many people see stablecoins as a trendier wallet product or a more exciting Web3 theme. However, after working with the HKMA, it becomes clear that regulators don't see it that romantically. In the eyes of regulators, stablecoins are primarily a new type of liability instrument, linked to reserves, redemption, anti-money laundering, payments, clearing, and consumer protection. This is precisely why the first batch of licensed institutions will naturally favor those least prone to error, most trustworthy, and most capable of undertaking bank-level regulation. Therefore, if we must first answer "why were the first batch of licenses given to large banks," the answer isn't so dramatic. It's not because stablecoins inherently belong to banks, but because in the early stages of the system, banks best fit the regulatory logic of "stability first, then expansion." The initial issuance of licenses addresses a safe start, not the final stage of the industry. The truly interesting question is never "why banks first," but rather "what kind of ecosystem will emerge in Hong Kong after banks enter the market first." From this perspective, the Hong Kong Monetary Authority (HKMA) has essentially written the question itself. Its initial focus on key scenarios includes not only cross-border and local payments, but also tokenized asset trading, collateral management, and programmable payments. In other words, what Hong Kong issued this time is not just a license to "do payments," but an access permit to enter on-chain financial infrastructure. This difference is crucial. Because once you shift your perspective from "payments" to "on-chain finance," many seemingly disparate things suddenly become connected. Let's first look at the statements from the two licensed institutions themselves. Anchorpoint, led by Standard Chartered, publicly stated that HKDAP is planned to be launched in phases starting from the second quarter of 2026, adopting a B2B2C model and entering the public market through authorized distributors. Its first roadmap also includes cross-border capital and payment flows, as well as the settlement and distribution of tokenized real-world assets. HSBC has also stated that it will launch a Hong Kong dollar stablecoin in the second half of 2026 and plans to integrate it with PayMe and the HSBC HK App, initially covering P2P, merchant payments, and tokenized investments. In other words, even the issuer itself didn't interpret this as simply "issuing one Hong Kong dollar first," but rather actively emphasized distribution, settlement, and asset transfer. This confirms my previous assessment that Hong Kong stablecoins don't need to compete with US dollar stablecoins for global dominance, nor do they need to replicate the path taken by the United States. Its more appropriate position is as the landing layer of Hong Kong's local currency within the on-chain financial system, the "last link" after the US dollar stablecoin completes its cross-border journey, and the most natural settlement currency when Hong Kong local assets enter the on-chain circulation. The first batch of licenses was issued today, and this judgment remains unchanged; in fact, it's become clearer. The only change is that what was previously a structural judgment is now entering the implementation phase. II. Referencing the US Ecosystem: Stablecoins are not payment plugins, but rather the cash leg of on-chain finance. To understand how to judge the implementation phase, the best reference point is not Hong Kong itself, but the United States. Because the stablecoin story that has truly taken off in the US today is no longer just about "paying for a cup of coffee with stablecoins." In July 2025, the GENIUS Act was signed into law, initially incorporating stablecoin payments into the federal institutional framework. By April 2026, White House research documents had explicitly discussed its impact on the banking system based on 1:1 reserves, the scope of eligible assets, and limits on stablecoin yields. Meanwhile, the broader digital asset market structure legislation, the CLARITY Act, is still progressing, but it remains stuck between the banking and crypto industries regarding stablecoin yields/rewards. The first issue the US addressed was "how to formally integrate payment stablecoins into the dollar system"; a larger rewriting of the market structure is still underway. However, the market didn't wait for all the legislation to be finalized before taking action. Following its acquisition of Bridge, Stripe launched Stablecoin Financial Accounts in 2025, allowing businesses in 101 countries to directly hold dollar-denominated stablecoin account balances and move funds between the stablecoin and traditional financial systems. In the same year, Stripe launched Open Issuance through Bridge, allowing companies to issue and manage their own stablecoins. The most important point here is not that "Stripe supports stablecoins," but that Stripe has advanced stablecoins from a payment method to the account, treasury, and product layers. Coinbase took a different, more representative path. It merged the original Coinbase Commerce into Coinbase Business, and deeply recognized that today, businesses are truly using stablecoins not just for receiving payments, but for a combination of custody, fiat offramps, payouts, accounting integrations, and payments. Taking it a step further, Coinbase launched x402 in 2025, sparking the narrative of aggregator commerce by placing stablecoin payments directly at the HTTP layer, allowing APIs, apps, and AI agents to natively complete small payments. Seeing this, it becomes clear that the discussion in the US today is no longer about whether "crypto payments can succeed," but rather whether stablecoins can become the underlying cash layer for internet-native transactions and business operations. Visa's actions also illustrate this point. Instead of becoming a new issuer itself, it continues to embed stablecoins into its core settlement network. In December 2025, Visa announced the launch of USDC settlement in the United States and disclosed that its annualized stablecoin settlement volume had exceeded $3.5 billion. Currently, through deep cooperation with Stripe and Bridge, and by joining the x402 Foundation, Visa has also incorporated stablecoins and the AI ​​payment ecosystem based on stablecoins into its network of networks. Stablecoins often do not replace the old network, but rather rewrite the form of currency circulating in the old network. The network itself may not be disrupted; what is rewritten first is the monetary layer. Looking deeper, payments aren't even the most interesting part of the US stablecoin ecosystem. The truly significant changes are on the asset side. In March 2024, BlackRock launched BUIDL, placing money market fund shares on its public blockchain. Franklin Templeton's Franklin OnChain U.S. Government Money Fund had assets of approximately $844 million by the end of March 2026, with at least 99.5% invested in U.S. government securities, cash, and repurchase agreements fully collateralized by U.S. government securities or cash. DTCC, after receiving a no-action letter from the SEC in December 2025, plans to launch a tokenization service for DTC-custodied real-world assets in the second half of 2026, covering the Russell 1000, major index ETFs, and U.S. Treasury bonds. Looking at these together, you'll find that what emerged in the US wasn't just a "stablecoin race," but a complete on-chain financial stack. Stablecoins act as on-chain cash, tokenized funds and treasuries as on-chain assets, market infrastructure handles registration, circulation, and custody, and payment, collateralization, clearing, and distribution grow on top of that. This is why I think that if our analysis of Hong Kong stablecoins only focuses on "why the first batch of licenses were issued to banks," our perspective is too narrow. What Hong Kong is truly vying for today is not just stablecoin payments, but rather the local currency interface, settlement gateway, and clearing position in the era of on-chain finance. Stablecoins are just the first brick. The real structure will not be merely a payment channel. III. The True Future of Hong Kong Dollar Stablecoins Lies in the Ecosystem. Of course, pessimists will naturally focus on the negative aspects. They might feel that giving the first batch to banks precisely demonstrates that Hong Kong has stifled innovation. Those best suited to maintain the old system may not be the most motivated to open up a new one. This concern is not unfounded. Mature institutions often possess neither technological nor compliance capabilities; what they truly lack is the willingness to rewrite their value chain. They are certainly willing to absorb new technologies into the old system, but they may not be willing to proactively promote a new path that could dilute their existing advantages. However, this pessimistic argument is only half true. The other half is that on-chain finance doesn't grow out of a vacuum. Without a trustworthy local currency settlement layer, without a compliant redemption mechanism, and without an entity capable of handling large-scale transactions and regulatory responsibilities, the subsequent payment, RWA, and tokenization ecosystems can only remain at the demo and fundraising levels. The most valuable lesson to learn from the US today isn't that "startups beat banks first," but rather the redistribution of labor and cooperation among traditional financial institutions, payment infrastructure companies, and native crypto companies within the same ecosystem. Hong Kong's decision to hand over the first key to banks may not be the end of innovation, but more likely just laying the foundation for the most difficult stage. Precisely because of this, I believe that what's truly worth watching after the initial license issuance isn't who holds the license, but who can develop capabilities beyond those licenses. Anchorpoint's proactive move towards a B2B2C model demonstrates that the issuer understands that having a license does not equate to having users. OSL's official statement further emphasizes its ecosystem, welcoming the initial license issuance and positioning itself in distribution, liquidity, and infrastructure partnerships. This signal is significant. It indicates that the initial concentration of issuance rights does not guarantee a similar concentration of long-term value. The real differentiator in the future may not be the issuance side, but rather the distribution side, wallets, payment interfaces, trading platforms, custody, compliance middleware, and who can connect on-chain cash to real assets and real transactions. Ultimately, the hardest part about stablecoins has never been issuing them, but retaining them. Payment is instantaneous, but financial matters take a whole night. Whether a company is willing to use Hong Kong dollar stablecoins for a settlement today only indicates that it has channel value; whether a company is willing to leave a portion of its operating funds in Hong Kong dollar stablecoins overnight tomorrow indicates that it begins to have balance value. Once settlements begin to convert into retention, stablecoins will transform from payment instruments into financial infrastructure. Taking it a step further, only when this portion of on-chain cash begins to naturally flow into tokenized funds, tokenized bonds, RWA trading, and collateral management will Hong Kong's stablecoin ecosystem truly connect with the entire on-chain financial ecosystem. This is also the point I'm most concerned about when I saw the first batch of licenses issued in Hong Kong today. If Hong Kong dollar stablecoins are only understood as a faster payment method, their potential is not very high. Hong Kong's local retail market is not large, and its payment infrastructure is already quite mature; relying solely on the front-end payment experience cannot tell a sufficiently compelling story. However, if it's placed within a larger structure—as an on-chain settlement layer for local currency, connecting payments, treasury, RWA, tokenization, and cross-border capital flows—its significance changes entirely. It's no longer just a Web3 product, but part of Hong Kong's digital financial infrastructure. Therefore, after this first batch of licenses was issued, my judgment is actually clearer than before. Hong Kong's first batch of stablecoin licenses were indeed given to the most conservative parties first. This is typical of Hong Kong, and it makes sense. However, the true potential of a Hong Kong dollar stablecoin won't be determined by the initial batch of listings, but rather by its ability to develop into a local settlement layer within Hong Kong's on-chain financial ecosystem. Banks can open the door first; what truly determines the market's viability is whether it can later develop distribution channels, interfaces, assets, and usage frequency. The first step has finally been taken. The real competition is only just beginning.

data.title: Everyone's talking about oil, but what the world really needs might be tokens.
data.description:
Author: Bao Yilong, Wall Street Insights

Computing power shortage is becoming the most intractable bottleneck in the AI ​​boom era.

Over the past few months, with the explosive growth in demand for "intelligent agents" (AI), the supply of computing resources has lagged far behind the rate of consumption. From frequent outages to soaring prices, cracks are spreading in the most vulnerable link of this technological boom.

Los Angeles engineer and technology investor Ben Pouladian said:

Everyone is talking about oil, but I think what the world is really short of is tokens.

Recently, Anthropic's popular programming tool, Claude Code, has experienced frequent service outages. The company has begun limiting user token usage during peak weekday hours, but this has resulted in numerous user complaints. Meanwhile, OpenAI has chosen to shut down its video generation application, Sora, to free up computing power to support its next-generation AI models. Cloud computing provider CoreWeave has raised its service prices by over 20% and is requiring smaller customers to sign long-term contracts of at least three years. The impact of this computing power crisis on the AI ​​industry is already clearly visible. Just as millions of enterprise users are deeply embedding AI tools into their workflows, the reliability and availability of these tools are declining simultaneously. The resulting loss of trust may become an unbearable burden for leading AI companies in the fierce competition for users. Tokens, a New Scarce Resource Tokens are the basic unit for measuring computing power consumption in the AI ​​field, directly corresponding to the computational overhead behind each model inference. On OpenAI's API platform, token calls surged from 6 billion per minute in October last year to 15 billion per minute at the end of March this year, an increase of 150% in less than six months. The core driving force behind this growth is the rapid popularization of "agent" AI. These tools can autonomously perform tasks, from writing software code to arranging property viewings for real estate agents, profoundly replacing workflows previously done manually. Ben Pouladian points out: AI is no longer just a chatbot that lets us ask for a recipe while standing in front of the refrigerator; it's orchestrating tasks, it's becoming smarter. Outages and rate limiting hit Anthropic hardest. Since mid-February this year, Anthropic's service outages have become increasingly frequent, and some enterprise customers have begun migrating workloads to other AI providers. As of April 8th, Anthropic's Claude API had an uptime of only 98.95% over the past 90 days. This figure falls far short of industry standards, which typically require software service providers to commit to 99.99% availability. Amir Haghighat, co-founder and CTO of AI inference startup Baseten, bluntly stated: AWS, databases, and payment platforms all require extremely high availability. However, the reality in the AI ​​field is not like that, and this falls far short of the quality of service you would expect from a company that provides intelligent capabilities for your applications. David Hsu, founder and CEO of software development platform Retool, said that he initially preferred using Anthropic's Opus 4.6 model to drive the company's AI agent tools, believing it performed best in enterprise scenarios, but recently switched to OpenAI's model. He said: Anthropic has been constantly down. Facing pressure, Anthropic announced at the end of March that it would limit token usage during peak hours from 5 a.m. to 11 a.m. Pacific Time on weekdays. This move quickly sparked a wave of complaints from users on social media. One user wrote on the X platform: "I haven't reached the Claude Code limit for several weeks, but this week I reached the limit in about 45 minutes." Ironically, the frequent outages occurred against the backdrop of Anthropic's rapid business growth. The company's annualized revenue jumped from $9 billion at the end of 2025 to $14 billion in just two months, and then doubled again to $30 billion. Prices Soar, GPU Rentals Surge 48% in Two Months Supply shortages have simultaneously driven up computing power costs. According to the computing power price index released by New York-based data service provider Ornn, the hourly cost of renting Nvidia's latest generation Blackwell chips is currently $4.08, a 48% increase from $2.75 two months ago. Vultr CEO J.J. Kardwell described the current situation as "a massive computing power shortage unseen in over five years." He explained: The problem is, why not just deploy more equipment? Because delivery cycles are too long, data center construction cycles are too long, and all available power capacity until 2026 has already been pre-booked. Bank of America analysts reinstated their coverage of CoreWeave at the end of last month, giving it a "buy" rating and predicting that demand for the company's services will continue to exceed supply until at least 2029. OpenAI CFO Sarah Friar also admitted in a recent public investor video interview: "I have indeed spent a lot of time trying to find any temporarily available computing power. We are currently making very difficult trade-offs on some things we wanted to move forward because of insufficient computing power." Historically, infrastructure can never catch up with demand. This dilemma is not unique to the AI ​​industry, but a classic proposition that runs through the history of technology. From the railway expansion of the 19th century to the telecommunications and internet bubble of the early 21st century, every technological boom has been accompanied by a lag in infrastructure development; demand growth has consistently outpaced resource acquisition and capacity expansion. Historical experience shows that price increases are often one of the few effective means of alleviating supply shortages. However, this option is particularly tricky for cutting-edge AI companies currently fiercely competing for users. Price increases could directly harm user growth, which is precisely the core pillar of their valuation logic. Finding a balance between maintaining service quality, controlling computing costs, and maintaining competitiveness will be the core challenge that the AI ​​industry must address in the next stage.

data.title: BTC targets $75,000; US-Iran negotiations reverse course again, could the market see a turnaround?
data.description:
Shaw, Jinse Finance

Starting in the early hours of April 14th, the crypto market rebounded strongly again. Bitcoin briefly broke through $74,000 this morning, reaching $74,904.2 at one point, a 24-hour increase of over 5%; Ethereum briefly broke through $2,300, reaching $2,394.50 at one point, a 24-hour increase of over 8.5%. Other major cryptocurrencies also rebounded to varying degrees.

Coinglass data shows that in the past 24 hours, a total of $531 million in positions were liquidated across the network, with $105 million in long positions and $426 million in short positions liquidated, mainly short positions. Among them, $229 million in Bitcoin short positions were liquidated and $136 million in Ethereum short positions were liquidated. In the past 24 hours, more than 177,200 people across the network have been liquidated.

Trump's statement that Iran remains willing to reach an agreement boosted investor risk appetite. US stocks closed higher, with the S&P 500 recovering all its year-to-date losses. US Treasury yields rose initially but then fell, and the dollar initially jumped due to safe-haven demand before declining throughout the day. Crude oil rose more than 9% at one point, but gains were limited by expectations of US-Iran peace talks. Currently, the news landscape is fraught with uncertainty, and the market remains focused on signals of diplomatic progress, stimulating a rise in risk assets. Global attention is now focused on the next steps in US-Iran negotiations. How will major asset markets react?

I. BTC Aims for $75,000, Crypto Market Rebounds Again, Risk Appetite Improves

Early this morning, the crypto market rebounded strongly again. Bitcoin surged rapidly this morning, briefly breaking through $74,000 and reaching $74,904.2, aiming for the $75,000 mark, with a 24-hour increase of over 5%. Ethereum also rebounded in tandem, briefly breaking through $2,300 and reaching $2,394.50, with a 24-hour increase of over 8.5%. Other major cryptocurrencies also rebounded to varying degrees.

According to Coinglass data, in the past 24 hours, a total of $531 million in positions were liquidated across the entire network, with $105 million in long positions and $426 million in short positions, primarily short positions. Specifically, $229 million in Bitcoin short positions were liquidated, and $136 million in Ethereum short positions were liquidated. Furthermore, in the past 24 hours, over 177,200 people across the network have been liquidated, with the largest single liquidation occurring in Aster - BTCUSDT, valued at $12.4072 million. Meanwhile, although US-Iran peace talks remained deadlocked, Trump indicated that Iran was still willing to reach an agreement, boosting investor risk appetite. US stock indices closed higher, with the S&P 500 recovering all its year-to-date losses. The S&P 500 rose 1.02%, the Dow Jones Industrial Average rose 0.63%, and the Nasdaq Composite rose 1.23%. US Treasury yields rose initially but then fell, with the 10-year yield falling 1.98 basis points and the 2-year yield falling 1.89 basis points. The US dollar initially jumped due to safe-haven demand but subsequently declined throughout the day, ultimately closing at its lowest level since March 3. Crude oil initially rose more than 9%, but gains were limited by expectations of US-Iran peace talks, and it subsequently fell back below $100. Gold fell for the second consecutive day, with gold futures falling more than 3% at one point. The breakdown of US-Iran peace talks, coupled with Trump's announcement of a news blackout, triggered widespread risk aversion. However, according to Xinhua News Agency early this morning, Trump told the media at the White House that the US received a call that morning from "the right person" in Iran who expressed a desire to reach an agreement. US officials revealed that contact between the US delegation and Iranian leaders continues after the Islamabad talks, and "progress is currently being made in striving to reach an agreement." Although the news is full of uncertainty, the market still chooses to focus on signals of diplomatic progress, stimulating the rise of some risk assets. In the crypto market, the continued push for regulatory legislation and the continuous inflow of institutional buying funds have also increased liquidity, which is beneficial to the market rebound. II. US-Iran Talks Reverse Again and Again, Stimulating Market Risk Appetite Sources revealed that the US and Iran are discussing holding a new round of face-to-face negotiations to seek a longer-term ceasefire agreement. The source indicated that the goal is to hold a new round of talks before the two-week ceasefire agreement announced on April 7 expires next week. They mentioned that one option is to return to Islamabad for a second round of negotiations, but other locations are also under discussion. According to RIA Novosti, citing the American magazine *The Atlantic*, the next round of "direct negotiations" between the US and Iran may be held on the 16th in Islamabad, the capital of Pakistan. Previous negotiations, chaired by US Vice President Vance in Islamabad, failed to achieve a breakthrough. However, in an interview with Fox News, Vance stated that the US has made significant progress in negotiations with Iran. When asked if there would be more negotiations, Vance said the ball was in Tehran's court. He added that the US expects Iran to make progress on opening the Strait of Hormuz and warned that the situation would change if Tehran did not do so. Vance stated that the US delegation withdrew from negotiations after determining that Iranian negotiators lacked the authority to reach a final agreement, adding that any progress now depends on the approval of the Tehran leadership. Vance emphasized that Trump remains open to establishing a more normalized relationship with Iran, provided key conditions are met. However, he stated that for Iran to become an economically normal country, it must become a normal country that does not pursue nuclear weapons and also a normal country that does not engage in "terrorism." Iran, however, stated that the US's constantly changing demands during negotiations in Islamabad were the reason for the failure to reach an agreement. The Iranian Foreign Ministry stated on the 13th that Iranian Foreign Minister Araghchi, in a phone call with French Foreign Minister Barro on the evening of the 12th, said that despite Iran's "complete distrust" of the US, it participated in the negotiations in a responsible and goodwill manner and made progress on several issues. US President Trump hinted earlier this Monday that he was open to more dialogue, stating that Iran had proactively contacted the US. According to Xinhua News Agency, Trump told reporters at the White House on the 13th that Iran had called the US that morning, saying "they want to reach an agreement." However, Trump also stated that he would not accept any agreement that would allow Tehran to possess nuclear weapons. "Iran will not have nuclear weapons," Trump told reporters at the White House. "We cannot allow one country to blackmail or extort the world." The Iranian nuclear issue and uranium enrichment are among the focal points of the US-Iran standoff. According to AXIOS, a US official and a source familiar with the matter revealed that the US proposed a 20-year moratorium on uranium enrichment for Iran during negotiations in Islamabad over the weekend. Iran, however, proposed a shorter period, a single-digit number of years. Sources said the US demand for a moratorium on uranium enrichment was a key issue in these marathon negotiations. "The US proposed at least a 20-year moratorium, with various other restrictions attached," said a source familiar with the matter. The US also demanded that Iran remove all highly enriched uranium from the country. Iran stated that it was willing to accept a "monitored de-enrichment process" for these materials. Although the previous round of US-Iran negotiations did not yield significant results, recent statements from all parties indicate that the negotiations have progressed beyond expectations. The market is focusing on these positive developments, risk appetite has increased again, and this has further stimulated the rise of risk assets such as cryptocurrencies. III. The CLARITY Bill Progress Accelerates, Prospects Gradually Become Clearer On April 14, White House digital asset advisor Patrick Witt stated that the previously considered "intractable" differences in crypto legislation have significantly decreased, and the prospects for passing the relevant bill are improving. With Congress reconvening, the Senate's key committee is expected to vote on the CLARITY bill, paving the way for further legislation. This bill aims to clarify the regulatory division of labor between the SEC and CFTC and establish a unified regulatory framework for exchanges and market participants. The current focus of the controversy remains on stablecoin incentive mechanisms. While the passed stablecoin bill prohibits issuers from directly paying interest to users, disagreements persist regarding whether third-party platforms should be allowed to provide rewards. Overall, policy-making is shifting from "high uncertainty" to "gradual convergence," and the regulatory framework for the crypto industry is expected to enter a phase of substantial progress. Furthermore, according to Hodler's Digest, Coinbase CEO Brian Armstrong publicly stated that after months of legislative delays and multi-party negotiations, the time is "right" for the passage of the US CLARITY Act. This statement represents a significant reversal of his stance on the bill. In January 2026, he twice publicly withdrew his support for the CLARITY Act, citing flaws in its provisions, directly causing the Senate Banking Committee to postpone its review and voting process, bringing the legislative process to a standstill. After months of bipartisan negotiations and multi-party consultations between the crypto industry and the banking sector, Armstrong stated that the current version of the bill has become a "powerful bill" and explicitly called on Congress to push for its enactment. As the most important comprehensive regulatory bill for the crypto market to date, the CLARITY Act is gradually becoming clearer after a long period of multi-party negotiations. The accelerated progress of this bill is one of the major positive factors currently supporting market trends. IV. Listed treasury companies, as major institutional buyers, have consistently purchased large amounts of related crypto assets, which is a significant factor supporting the market. When the market is sluggish, institutional buying can alleviate downward pressure. And when the market rebounds strongly, institutional buying becomes a major boost to asset prices. V.
